%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 3.2 Useful Signal Operations

> **Section Objective:** Learn the discrete-time operations that move or stretch a sequence without changing the sample values themselves.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- right shift / delay
- left shift / advance
- integer expansion

## 1. Shifting a Discrete-Time Signal

Shifting moves the entire sequence along the \(n\)-axis. Every sample keeps its original height — only its index location changes.

**The danger is the sign inside the brackets.** When you replace \(n\) by \(n - M\) with \(M > 0\), the sequence shifts **right** (delay). When you replace \(n\) by \(n + M\) — equivalently, \(M < 0\) — the sequence shifts **left** (advance).

### MINIMAL EXAMPLE

Suppose \(x[n]\) has a nonzero sample at \(n = 2\). After applying \(y[n] = x[n - 5]\), that same sample now appears at \(n = 7\). The height is unchanged; only the location moved right by 5.

## 2. Integer expansion

This is the textbook's delay example. Here \(x_s[n]\) is the shifted sequence and \(x[n]\) is the original. Subtracting 5 inside the argument means every sample appears 5 index positions later — a right shift by 5.

**Exam trigger:** When the expression has \(n\) minus a positive integer, choose a right shift.

**Common misuse:** Students often think the minus sign means left. For signal arguments, \(n - M\) with \(M > 0\) is a delay — the sequence moves to the right.

$$x_s[n] = x[n - 5]$$

$$y[n] = x[n - M]$$
*This is the general discrete-time shift rule. \(M\) is an integer shift amount.

- If \(M > 0\): \(y[n]\) is \(x[n]\) shifted **right** by \(M\) (delay).
- If \(M < 0\): \(y[n]\) is \(x[n]\) shifted **left** by \(|M|\) (advance).

**When to use it:** Whenever an exam asks for a delayed or advanced sequence.

**Trap:** Read \(M\) from the whole argument. For example, \(x[n + 3] = x[n - (-3)]\), so \(M = -3\) and this is a **left shift by 3**, not a right shift.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="eyJjcmFtIjoiRHJhZyBNIGFuZCBtZW1vcml6ZTogcG9zaXRpdmUgTSBtb3ZlcyB0aGUgc3RlbXMgcmlnaHQuIiwic3RhbmRhcmQiOiJDb21wYXJlIHRoZSBvcmlnaW5hbCBhbmQgc2hpZnRlZCBzdGVtIHBsb3RzIHNhbXBsZSBieSBzYW1wbGUuIiwidG9wX3Njb3JlIjoiVGVzdCBuZWdhdGl2ZSBNIHZhbHVlcyBhbmQgZXhwbGFpbiB3aHkgeFtuICsgM10gaXMgYW4gYWR2YW5jZS4ifQ==" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Integer Expansion

Expansion by an integer factor \(L\) does **not** make sample heights larger. Instead, it spreads the original samples farther apart on the \(n\)-axis and fills all newly created intermediate positions with zero.

### MINIMAL EXAMPLE

For \(L = 3\), the original samples \(x[0]\), \(x[1]\), \(x[2]\) move to \(n = 0\), \(n = 3\), \(n = 6\). The positions \(n = 1, 2, 4, 5\) all become zero.

> **Expansion changes sample locations, not sample amplitudes.**

#### Note
Expansion is sometimes called upsampling or zero-insertion. The sample values are copied exactly — no multiplication occurs.

$$x_e[n] = \begin{cases} x[n/L], & n = 0, \pm L, \pm 2L, \ldots \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
***Equation (3.2) — Expansion by an integer factor.**

- \(x_e[n]\) is the expanded sequence.
- \(L\) is a positive integer expansion factor.
- \(x[n/L]\) is evaluated **only** when \(n\) is an integer multiple of \(L\); at all other indices the output is zero.

**When to use it:** When an exam says expand, upsample, or insert zeros by a factor \(L\).

**Common misuse 1:** Do not multiply sample amplitudes by \(L\). The formula copies values, it does not scale them.

**Common misuse 2:** Do not evaluate \(x[n/L]\) when \(n/L\) is not an integer — those positions are defined as zero.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Right shift (delay): \(x_s[n] = x[n - 5]\) moves every sample 5 positions right; heights unchanged.
- General shift rule: \(y[n] = x[n - M]\); positive \(M\) delays, negative \(M\) advances — the sign inside the brackets decides direction.
- Expansion formula (3.2): \(x_e[n] = \begin{cases} x[n/L], & n = 0, \pm L, \pm 2L, \ldots \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\) — copies samples to multiples of \(L\), zeros elsewhere.
- Expansion inserts zeros between samples; it never multiplies amplitudes by \(L\).

*Next, these operations become building blocks for describing more complicated discrete-time signals.*
